Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Question #19 came from Phillip who wanted to know how to improve his process for
creating information products.
Kind of a tough question to answer since I don’t really know what Phillip’s process is but
there are some things to think about in that area. Believe it or not I’m constantly amazed.
I was talking to a friend of mine yesterday, whose name you’d know. He’s a big internet
guru. We were chuckling about how when we share ideas with people, whether in
conferences or webinars like this, quite often the people we’re talking to will be blown
away by what we consider to be a very simple idea.
For example, I was talking to a group of business people the other day and told them that
the most important thing they need to do is keep a list of their customers. There were
several people who were just blown away by that advice. It was absolutely brilliant; keep a
record of who buys from you and what.
The last time I talked to somebody about information publishing it happened to be Parris
Lampropoulos, who most of you know is a high six figure copywriter. In fact, I think Parris
easily does over a million dollars a year in royalties.
Parris was telling me about his first experience in this particular area. He made some of
the classical blunders and we’ll talk about those in a minute.
There are key questions you have to answer if you’re going to get into the information
publishing industry and first of all is identify your niche. You need to find an area where
you believe you bring tremendous value to people. Sometimes that’s determined for us by
a client for example. Obviously your first question is what am I going to write about?
Once you determine that then the next question needs to be, “Who’s the best prospect
for this kind of a report?” Let’s assume it’s a report at this point. We’ll talk a little bit about
format in a minute.
Who’s my best prospect and where will I find them? Will I find them on a direct mail list
or e-list? Will I find them as subscribers to an online ezine? Are they registrants to a
membership website? Where are these guys online?
If you’re anything like me you probably hate the whole idea of pay per click and SEO
(Search Engine Optimization) as a way to acquire prospects because they’re so passive.
You bid on a keyword and then have to wait for somebody to type that keyword in.
If you’re doing Search Engine Optimization it’s the same thing. You have to wait for
someone else to decide they want what it is you’re selling or talking about. It’s just too
passive – for me anyway – and so much of the control over your marketing is lost as a

-- 1 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page 2 of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
result.
I would want to find a product area where there are mailing lists available, websites or
ezines that I can partner with. Ones that would give me more control over the outbound
messaging, where I can proactively go to prospects rather than wait for them to discover
that they need me.
Get a feel for where your prospects are hiding and then the most cost efficient way to
reach that prospect. For a lot of us that will be online because it is so cheap.
I have clients who have basically done all the SEO work and all the PPC work and then
they’re sitting around wondering why their companies are not growing very quickly, or as
quickly as they should be.
There’s a cost involved with free internet and that cost is time and time is money. If you’re
sitting around waiting for people to find you and doing everything you can to be findable on
the web you’re going to get a tiny fraction of the attention and the readership and response
you would get if you go into the mail.
So a lot of times even paying $400 or $500 or $600 or even $1,000 to go into the mail
is the answer if it’s going to bring you that business faster. Cost efficiency is more than
just how can it be done on the cheap. It’s a calculation that includes the revenue and the
profits that you can generate by being more proactive.
A good example would a company that’s sitting here making 100 sales per month using
PPC and SEO. They have no media costs and the profit margin is very high on those
sales. However, there’s a mailing list out there full of people who are just like his prospects.
They have bought products like this before. They’ve purchased with credit cards and
bought as a result of a direct mailing. If he would just mail that list he could get hundreds
or even thousands of additional orders. Although the margin on each order would be
smaller he would still end up making a lot more money.
After you’ve identified who your prospect is and how you’re reaching him you need also to
know what is his most pressing problem or desire relative to the subject area you’re going
into.
That’s what will activate people. It’s what will stop them in their tracks if you have it in the
headline. It’s what’s going to cause them to read your sales message. Connecting with
that problem or desire is going to be what gets them to ultimately click your order button or
call your toll free number or return your response device.
Identify what it is that is motivating him now. In the financial markets right now there

-- 2 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
are two things going on at the top of the news. We know that people are worried about
the economic slowdown and the recession and how bad it can get. We also know that
gold just hit a new all-time record high today. Oil, copper, platinum, and palladium are all
skyrocketing. Commodity investors are getting rich.
Your prospect could have two completely opposite emotions right now. He could be
terrified that he’s going to lose his job and the economy and stock market is going to
crash, while at the same time he’s well aware because it’s at the top of every newscast
that gold is going through the roof. So he’s feeling greedy about that but also feeling fear
about the state of the economy.
I was having this conversation with a client this morning. He was trying to decide what
type of product he next wants to promote in this environment. Does he want to go with
a product that relieves people of their fear or give people a product that allows him to
harness a hot trend in this market?
We defaulted to go for the greed in this situation. There will be a time in the near future
where the fear is overwhelming – when we see the stock market drop 700 to 900 points in
a day. For right now the stock market is going sideways. The really dynamic movement is
in this other area.
You need to figure out what the most powerful and motivating emotion is going to be in
that prospect. Then you need to decide what you can deliver that will solve that problem or
fulfill that desire. This is about bringing value to people’s lives.
As we’ll see in a moment the worst mistake you can make as an information marketer is
to sell something on the front end. Bring in a new customer in, selling him something that
really doesn’t bring value. There are a lot of people who do this. They write really red hot
promotions for a product, whether it’s an information product or something else, but when
you get the product it disappoints.
The biggest risk or cost in terms of both time and money is that new customer acquisition
promotion. That’s the biggest risk you’ll ever take when you get into this business. You can
go out there and spend time and money developing a promotion and have it fall flat on its
face. So you have capital risk as well as the risk of time.
If it does fall on its face that means the time you spent developing that you weren’t
developing another promotion, product or another business. Then there is lost opportunity
costs as well.
The easiest thing to do is once the customer is online is selling to him. You have a
customer in your data base that has already bought from you, likes you and had a good
experience with your product so to sell to him is the easiest thing to do.

-- 3 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
That’s why when you go to a direct mail promotion and you’re trying to acquire new
customers for a $100 product you’ll probably be pretty happy with a half a percent
response rate. With such a response rate you could break even at that level.
When you’re selling to a customer that has already bought from you a half a percent would
be a disaster. You’d expect 3 to 10 times that much. On house file promotions I’ve seen as
high as a 10% response rate. That’s 20 times what you get in the mail when you go out to
get a new customer.
That’s the value of having those customers on your file. It makes no sense to go out and
spend all that money and take all that risk and then deliver a product that will make your
customer file unresponsive later because they’ve already learned that you don’t deliver
where you promise.
Point number four here is you absolutely have to make sure if anything you over deliver in
the product. You under promise in the promotion and you over deliver in the product.
You need to ask yourself what product format your prospect has shown he’s willing to
accept. This is fairly easy to do. If you’re doing direct mail you go to the library, you get the
most current Standard Rate and Data Service book. You sit down at a table and look up
your niche or industry, your competitors, and see what those people are buying.
Are they buying books, special reports, cassette or DVD series, live events? What is it
they’re buying? That’s a psychographic fact about those prospects. They have sent the
message, “Hey, I feel comfortable acquiring information in this format.”
Here’s an example from the financial newsletter industry. People have made literally
billions of dollars selling investment newsletters to rich old white men. That’s what Agora,
Phillips, Weiss and KCI do.
However, the minute they try to sell the same newsletters to young people – people
younger than 50 – they’ve gotten their heads handed to them. Why? Because people
younger than 50 like getting their information in other ways.
They don’t want to read a dull, boring black ink on white paper newsletter. They want to
read a glossy magazine, so they get Kiplinger, Forbes, Fortune or another magazine.
Maybe they get it online.
Understanding the product’s format that your prospect prefers is very important. He is
telling you this is how he likes to absorb his information.
Number six, can you achieve your objective in a single installment or would a

-- 4 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
series be more effective?
This gets back to the product again. You need to decide whether your topic is a book or
newsletter. One of the classic squib moves I see young people make when they first get
a job at a financial publisher happens in the meeting where people are sitting around
brainstorming new product ideas.
Nine times out of ten the youngest, newest person in the room will either suggest we do an
investment newsletter for women or we do an investment newsletter on some subject that
doesn’t change with time.
The reason why an investment newsletter for women is not a brilliant idea is that women
invest in the same stocks that men do. Thus they need the same information that men do.
Nobody has to date fielded a successful investment newsletter specifically for women,
although many people have tried. When I’m in those meetings I simply say, “Tell me how
investing is different for a woman than for a man. If you can do that then I think we should
do it.”
It all comes down to the same decisions they all have to make. They may feel differently
about things, but the bottom line is a good stock is good and a bad stock is bad and that’s
pretty much it.
A lot of times the second mistake is that they’ll come in with an idea that would make for a
really great book. The information that’s going to be presented doesn’t need to be updated
every day or month. The topic is static. If that’s the case, you want a static product.
If the information is dynamic and changing all the time, if your advice or the techniques
used to solve the problem or achieve a desired result changes as time goes on then you
may want some type of continuing product. If you’re trying to get a person from point A to
point B in terms of their understanding of a subject then maybe it’s a course. Maybe it’s a
course on investment.
My daughter’s really into horses and I saw an interesting show on television last night.
There’s this guy named Perelli and he goes around the country showing people – he’s
kind of like the dog whisperer, only for horses. He has courses where he teaches you to
improve your horse’s behavior that sells for thousands and thousands of dollars.
That could be a book. It’s probably not a newsletter because the dynamics don’t change
that much from month to month. You can deliver all of this information at one time. It’s
either a book or the information is so exhaustive that it can’t be absorbed in a few hundred
pages of a book, then maybe it’s a course.

-- 5 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Finally, what is he willing to pay? This is again the idea of market research. Go grab that
SRDS or go to the websites that you’re going to be competing against or whose prospects
are similar to yours.
Open an Excel spreadsheet and start entering in there what types of products are they
buying and what are they charging for them? Are they books, newsletters, magazines,
ezines, courses, how much do they charge? Are they continuity programs or are they cash
up front programs? How do they work?
As you work your way through that process and you already know what niche or basically
what you want to write about, your product will start taking shape in your mind. There are
some classic errors.
I mentioned the classic error that Parris made when he attempted to become an
information publisher many years ago. You get all excited about your information product,
“Oh, man, this is going to be great!” You develop the product then you go out there and
you start promoting it.
Sure enough, you’re bringing in new customers at a profit, but not much of a profit
because it costs you money to bring in new customers. The thing then runs its course and
you’re sitting there when the dust settles with the final realization that you made very little
money at the end of the day for all that work.
Instead of looking at an information product as an opportunity to make a whole lot
of money, think of it as the beginning of a business. Think of it as the beginning of a
relationship with the new customers that you’re going to generate.
The three things I have on the screen right now are things you need to think about. Let’s
say we’re doing this online and I’ve just read your sales letter. I love what you’ve got to
offer and I decide to buy it.
I click Order Now and I go to your sales page. On that sales page I’m going to fill out my
credit card information and all that good stuff. On that sales page you could also have a
forced free trial on there, a little box with a check mark and it can be pre-checked if you
want that says “Please also include the three free month subscription to blank, blank.
I understand if I like it I’ll do nothing and you’ll invoice me at the rate of X, otherwise I’ll
cancel in my first 90 days and owe nothing.”
That’s free money. The guy’s already ordering and this is the moment in time when
he’s happiest with you and most excited about his product. Having a forced free trial
for a secondary product is a great way to go there. He clicks Send or Order and then
most people will throw up a thank you page. Why not instead present him with another
opportunity to buy?

-- 6 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You’ve got his credit card information and script can be written so you offer him a second
product. Maybe it’s the same product again for a friend. Maybe it’s the advance version
of this product or something else. All he has to do is click a button on that page and he’s
automatically billed for that product as well.
At the very least, third item here, have a secondary product ready to go. Even if you have
a bump, a forced free trial, both of these happen at the time of ordering. Now you’ve got
a customer and what are you going to do with him next month? This is the importance of
thinking of this as a business and not as a one shot deal.
It takes a little extra time to have a bump product and a forced free trial product and to
have a secondary product that you’re going to offer them next month. Think about yourself
down the line. You’ve got 1,000 or 2,000 new customers and there you sit with your face
hanging out. What are you going to do with them?
You go through this process and now you’re forming a business. A very profitable one, too,
if you go about it the right way. So I hope that helped.
In slow economic times people are going to be looking for more ways
to make money. What are the key psychological triggers you hit in down
economic times? In times like this all of these things are happening
simultaneously in all of us.
Yesterday we got some bad job numbers. We also got some bad inflation
numbers. We got both good and bad earnings numbers, too. There’s a feeling
of fear. What’s going to happen?
You know what? Every one of us does the worst case scenario thing. Maybe it’s when
we’re just falling asleep at night or we’re not otherwise engaged. We begin playing with
what’s the worst that could happen because of this situation. We play this movie in our
minds of the absolute worst that can happen. All of us are doing it all the time.
We’re doing it with relationships, with business, investments and with our jobs. So fear is
constantly present. It’s more active, compelling and motivating at a time when we’re taken
out of the “what if” and put into the now, the present time, when something really bad is
happening.
A lot of the economic situation we’re facing right now, we’re hoping it won’t be as bad as
some people say it might be and it probably won’t. We’re certainly hoping it won’t touch us
personally. But it wouldn’t take much to really activate that fear in terms of bad news in the
marketplaces.

-- 7 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
A second one is helplessness and this is a big one. One of the very top causes of
depression and suicide is a feeling that we’re not in control of our own destiny. Again, this
question was really couched in economic terms.
There are millions of people out there who go to work, work hard all day and have been
for decades, and their immediate future is not within their control. If the economy hammers
their company particularly hard or if their company is not well managed and prepared for a
time like this, they could lose their jobs. They have no control over that, so helplessness is
a big motivator. We all hate that feeling.
Third is resentment or anger. Right now it’s election season so it’s real easy to find people
to be mad at because we’re thinking about politicians anyway. You’re not going to hear
me say a lot of bad things about the President, but the administration in Washington is
responsible for what is happening now in the real estate market. There’s absolutely no
doubt about that.
The administration lowered interest rates making houses extremely cheap to buy. This
drove up demand and caused the supply to drop and therefore caused prices to rise. At
the same time they did not regulate the lending industry.
They allowed all kinds of abuses to happen where people were allowed to buy more home
than they could afford just so the banks could make more money. This was a mistake. If
you want to be mad at somebody over this, know who to be mad at.
At a time like this there is also a desire for security. We’re afraid and feel unsettled. We
know that just anything that could happen. At a time like this you start daydreaming about
what it would be like to never have to worry about money again.
Number five is really an offshoot of that -- greed. Hey, if I had a million dollars in the bank
I wouldn’t have to be feeling fearful, helpless, resentful and angry. The solution to my
situation is to become independently wealthy.
So depending on what your product is, hopefully if you have a product that helps people
make money it connects with all of these. The purpose of testing it in direct mail and online
is to find out which kind of a lead is going to give you the greatest readership.
I hope that answers the question. These are the five things that you need to be hitting on
right now very hard.
By the way, this resentment and anger thing is like a two-edged sword. In the financial
markets right now most of our prospects are conservative, older men. Going to them and
reminding them of how we got into this mess is not going to make you any friends.

-- 8 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If that’s your market, going with, “The White House got us into this but can’t get us out of
it,” is just going to basically get you killed in the mail. Be sensitive as to who you’re talking
to when you use all of these things.
We had a thing recently where we began hiring some copywriters. One of the things we
said in our ad was that you must be motivated by money. As if to prove they hadn’t read
the ad when they filled out the application there was a question on there, “What really
angers you?” A bunch of the people that filled out the application said greed.
Obviously they hadn’t read the ad. I want greedy people. I want people who want to make
money and are willing to work for it. Understanding who you’re writing to is very important.
Several good questions here and we can move through them quickly.
Would it be better to position yourself in B2C or B2B markets right now?
It all depends on what the B2C market is and what the B2B market is. If I was working
the Business to Business markets right now I’d be wanting to work for companies that do
extremely well when inflation is taking off and when the economy is slowing down.
Who makes money at a time like that? Gold miners make money, rare coin dealers, pawn
shops, people that sell cosmetics and low cost personal luxury items make money. Stores
that sell discount products make money and those are the B2B markets I would want to be
in. Obviously the last wouldn’t be a store but a wholesaler.
Companies that sell products that save people money on essential activities, things that
they have to do even during a recession. Eating is an example of that. There are areas in
the B2B markets that are going to be extremely good and those are the ones I’d want to
be in.
The same is true with Business to Consumer. If you’re with a company that sells Rolls-
Royces you’re probably going to do okay. People who can afford Rolls-Royces in good
times can also afford them in bad times for the most part.
If you’re selling family vacations to middle class people who are terrified of losing their
jobs, you’re probably not going to do real well. Think about the times, where we are right
now and select clients and promote clients on a basis of how well you think they’re likely to
fair as the economy slows.
Marcus is also planning to come here and wants advice on whether he should go into
English speaking markets now.
The answer is yes. Don’t give up your German and Swiss clients; just add a few English

-- 9 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page 0 of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
speaking markets. Add a client or two from the US, Australia or wherever. Build up your
sample portfolio and ease into this. You’ve got some time and you can have the best of
both worlds. You’re in a really good position.
Are we going towards times where long sales letters with lots of non-hype
information and valuable background are going to pull best, especially
outside the newsletter world? For example, the adventure capital, private
equity world.
I’ve got to tell you, Marcus, I kind of felt like this was a trick question. I know you’re not
trying to trick me. In the venture capital private equity world you’re dealing with regulated
industries. You’re limited in the first place in terms of hype and what you can say.
If your client is doing this correctly he’s got legal counsel. I don’t know what the rules are
in Germany where you are but over here we have a couple of organizations plus our own
compliance officers and attorneys who keep us from getting too hype-y.
In that sense and in this particular area of venture capital, private placement, and private
equity you have to be very cautious in terms of what you say. Make sure you have a good
attorney reviewing all your material.
Really I think the spirit of the question has to do with are advertorials going to continue
selling well and out pull other types of promotions?
There’s no real way to answer that across all industries, media, and product groups.
Essentially my answer would be that we’ve always been in that time. Since the 1970’s
when I started writing sales copy all of our promotions always delivered value to the
reader.
Rather than thinking of it as non-hype information, the idea was to deliver something, an
answer that the reader is looking for, at the beginning of your promotion. Then have your
promotion salt it with more helpful information or advice. That keeps them reading and it
demonstrates your expertise.
It’s a fantastic technique to use. As I said, I’ve been using it since the 70s. We call them
advertorials and the 24 page magalogs that you see in our swipe files and so forth are
good examples of those.
So, yes, and especially online today. If you are familiar with any of the online gurus like Jeff
Walker or Rich Schefren, one of the key points they’re trying to get people to understand
is that when you’re selling to consumers today there are three kinds of recommendations;
three kinds of things that are going to push them to buy from you.

-- 10 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
One is third-party proof. You go to that prospect and say, “Hey, you don’t know me from
Adam but I’ve got a great product and here’s all the things it does and buy it now.”
Then there’s when a friend comes to him and says on a blog or social forum network,
“Hey, have you seen Fred’s product? It’s really great. I’ve got one and it’s working great.”
Which of those two appeals is going to really work the best? The basic sales letter where
you go out and say, “Here’s my product, buy it now,” is always going to work. Any time you
can have someone else recommend your product for you, that’s going to work better. They
trust you’re a marketer.
You know how huge the “do not call” lists are? We all hate marketers in our own lives. You
know what happens in my house when a telemarketer calls late at night. I always let the
redhead handle them and it’s not a pretty sight to watch.
Marketers are not desirable guests in most people’s homes, so they try to avoid what we
send them. They try not to read it. They try to avoid us at every point. When we do get
through to them, they’re very skeptical about everything we say to them.
Wouldn’t it be better to have somebody else appear, a friend doing the recommending for
you? That’s a superior way to just going out and saying, “Buy my product.”
Another way to do it is become that friend. The way that you do that is by providing
information that has great value to your prospect for some period of time prior to allowing
him to buy anything. In that case you become the friend.
Advertorials are extremely powerful. Make sure that you’re not just giving them non-hype
information. Make sure that you are meeting a need in their life with your promotion.
Online feel especially free to do it in more than one step.
You can take a week or several weeks from the first time someone comes to you and do
nothing but shower them with content. It proves your expertise, it proves that you’re a
friend and advocate, that you have their best interests at heart. Then when you finally get
to the point where you do have a product to sell you don’t have to hype it.
You don’t have to make unrealistic claims or use a lot of exclamation points – slammers all
over the place. All you have to do is say you realize they’re interested in this and you have
this thing you just developed that does that. Why not take a look at it and click here. It can
be very simple then.
What you’re doing is serializing a sales letter or an advertorial in that sense. Instead of
delivering the whole thing in 24 pages through the mail, you’re delivering a lot of little
editorial things that deliver content over time and then allowing them to purchase. It’s a

-- 11 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page 2 of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
great way to sell and working better than pretty much everything online right now.
The average subscriber to The Total Package spends $5 every month with us. You
have some idea of what the ratio is of editorial content to promotion because you all are
members of the total package. You know that every day there is a long-form, valued added
article that goes to people. Every single day, five days a week, and then we have short
emails that go out when we have a new product that comes up. The ratio of promotion to
value added information is very important.
If the ratio is right maybe you’ll get to $5 per subscriber per month. The last time I checked
none of our competitors are anywhere close. We take great pride in that because to us
that doesn’t say, “Hey, man we write great promotional copy.” To us that says we write a
great daily ezine that brings value to people’s lives and that’s why they know we know
what we’re talking about. That’s why they know their interests are our priority and how they
know we can be trusted.
Stick with that concept, Marcus. You’re on the right track.
Steve wants to know:
Can you point me to some good resources on positioning?
I’ve read one book on positioning and it was all I needed. It’s made me millions. The book
is, Positioning - The Battle for Your Mind by Al Ries and Jack Trout. Amazon has it and I
strongly recommend it. Any of you who are copywriters or business owners and who aren’t
interested in positioning, my suggestion is that you get interested and read it. It’s made me
millions.
If you were a newbie copywriter what letter would you send out to get people
interested?
That’s a question that would require a lot more time than we have today. I did an entire
product on this, the Six-Figure Copywriting course that we have in our store where I lay it
all out.
I wanted to write for investment newsletters so I got an Oxford’s Directory, which is a list of
all the newsletters in the country. I went through it and every week I would mail 100 letters.
I had gone through it first and identified the companies I wanted to work with most and put
them in the order I wanted to contact them. Every week I would send out 100 letters to the
companies on the list, working from top to bottom.
In every one of those letters I would say, “Hi, I’m Clayton Makepeace, I write direct mail
and here’s what I’ve done and a couple of samples of my work. I’m opening my schedule

-- 12 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
right now for the second half of the year. If you’d like to talk to me about an assignment
please give me a call.” Down in the PS I’d say, “If I don’t hear from you by Wednesday I’ll
give you a call.”
There are all kinds of little permutations, tricks, and stuff that I added to that but that was
the basic bang. Then I got myself into the discipline of every morning when I got up I would
start banging the phones and calling those 100 people.
It could be 20 or 25 a day, whatever I could do, but I would call those people that I’d mailed
to the preceding week would be the ones I’d call this week. That was enough to get me
started.
All of this is like 1980. As I started to produce some winners for those people, word of
mouth got around. Who wrote this piece of Johnny Johnson’s mail? Who wrote this piece
that SRC is mailing? Slowly but surely word got out that it was Clayton.
One of the fun things that happened is there was a company I wanted to write for and they
didn’t know me from Adam. However, I had a promotion in their niche that was working
quite well. So I sent them a letter where I hand wrote, “My name is Clayton. I wrote this
and I’d like to create a package for you in November. Give me a call?”
The thing was delivered and the woman that received it called me immediately and said,
“We had an entire meeting yesterday trying to figure out who wrote this thing. You’re
hired.”
As you get some winners out there, everyone in your marketplace will see them and it’s
a lot of fun then when you can deliver a copy and say, “Hi, I wrote this and I’ve got a slot
open in three or four months. Send me $12,000 and it’s yours.” It’s fun to watch.
There are a lot of people who sit around and think, “Wow, I wish I was where Clayton was
making a quarter or half a million dollars a month. The money’s rolling in and he’s doing
great.” Especially times like right now I’m thinking about how I was getting started that I
envy where you guys are.
You’re at a point where the emotional rewards, as well as the financial rewards, if you stick
with it and make it happen are off the charts. Once you’re established and things are going
along pretty well it’s not nearly as exciting. It’s so much fun when you get those first few
jobs, getting that recognition and your name starts getting around.
It’s a really a great time. Mark my words, every one of you that does stick with it and do
this and persevere, every single one of you is going one day going to be where I am.
You’re going to be sitting here thinking – you’re going to be as old as me – “Wow, that was
fun. Those were good times and Makepeace was right. That was the best.”

-- 13 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Steve also wants to know: What do I do when I get stuck or hit a wall?
I quit. I get up from my desk, walk away, and watch TV or play video games. I chat with the
kids or my wife, take the motorcycle out for a ride, or get in the Porsche. I’m a big believer
in allowing my brain to solve the problems. So often I’ll be up against an intractable
problem – sometimes I just have to walk across the room.
Get out. Go to the bathroom, go pour myself a cup of coffee or something like that.
Disengage mentally. You’re not thinking or worrying about it. When you’re thinking about
something and trying to solve a problem your conscious mind is getting in the way. Your
subconscious is all about making connections.
That’s where creativity comes from. Get your conscious mind out of the way. Think about
something else. Thomas Edison liked to take naps. You need to disengage. That’s the
best thing I can tell you for when you hit a wall.
How many times do I edit my work?
I do it a lot. I’m working on a letter right now that I’ve been working on for three weeks in
my spare time. I’ve probably gone through five complete drafts. I work on it for a while and
then I set it aside for a couple of days and then I come back to it.
The answer to this, Steve, is it all depends on the project. There are projects where I sat
down and cranked it out in a day – a 24-page magalog copy. It worked great. Other times I
went through 20 or 25 drafts. I just wasn’t happy with it. It wasn’t smooth and didn’t flow. It
didn’t feel like it was resonating at a gut level with my prospects.
I know all of the other top writers are like this, Gary Bencivenga most notably. A client calls
asking when it will be ready. The answer is, “When it’s ready.”
I saw a great documentary on Michelangelo the other day. For those of you who know the
story I want to give you a heads up, I will screw this up. Essentially the Pope had told him
he wanted him to paint the Sistine Chapel ceiling. Michael’s response was, “Are you nuts?
I’m a sculptor not a painter. No, I’m not going to do it.”
The Pope says yes he is. Michael says no he’s not. The Pope says yes and eventually he
had no choice but to paint the ceilings. He gets up there, 5 ½ years into this and he hates
it. He’s a sculptor and wants to get back to marble. On top of all of that the Pope is coming
in like three times a week asking when it’s going to be done. This went on for 5 ½ years.
Finally Michelangelo gets ticked off and tells the Pope where he can stick the entire project
and says he’s going back to Florence. The Pope then had to go to Michelangelo asking

-- 14 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
him to please not quit the project. He’s sorry and would Michael please forgive him. He
begged him to come back and do the work.
I thought that was really instructive -- mark that up to another little side tangent there. It will
be done when it’s done. If the Pope would have listened to that and believed it in the first
place he wouldn’t have had to apologize later.
Who are your mentors now and in the past?
The world has changed a lot since I got started. We didn’t have mentors and protégés
back there in the 70s. When I started doing this it was up to you. You had to study what
other people were doing and mailing. There were a few books, the masters, that everyone
recommends: Hopkins, Caples, Ogilvy, but that was pretty much it.
I didn’t have any mentors. I was lucky enough to have a friend named Michael Angler that
you might call a mentor. He had been studying copywriting for some time and he was a
friend of the owner of the agency that I got my first copywriting job at. He was the one who
handed me Caples and Hopkins and the masters.
Other than that, most of what I learned was by trial and error or by studying what
successful companies were mailing. The same is true today. Honestly there is nobody
that I work with or that works with me. It’s not that I don’t think mentors are a great thing. I
love to be a mentor to other people, but it’s not within me. I wasn’t raised in this mentoring
environment. I’m sorry I can’t help you more with that one.
The key is – I’ve said it a million times and it’s old news – get on a lot of mailing lists and
read what the great copywriters are writing: Boardroom, Phillips, Agora, KCI, Rodale.
Read the great copywriters. The people who are writing for those companies are the jet
fighter pilots of our industry.
It’s not like the internet guru who says he wrote a promotion for himself and it pulled
really well. The other part of that statement is, “Yeah, but if I tested what I wrote against
somebody who really knew what they were doing, they would probably have kicked the
living daylights out of me in terms of response.”
Jet pilots of our industry are the ones who go up against each other day after day,
scrapping for half a point edge in response or a $5 or $10 advantage in average sale.
Those are the people who live and die by their work in a highly competitive environment.
When you subscribe to something Boardroom does or buy one of Rodale’s books and
start receiving that material, it’s solid gold. Those are the people who are at the very top of
their craft. Those are the best mentors you can have.

-- 15 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I was talking to Gary Bencivenga not too long ago and told him he was really the only
mentor I ever had. He didn’t know it because he had no idea who I was. I was about 10
years younger than him so he was already taking on the clients that I was dreaming about.
He was producing great winners.
Whenever I got a Bencivenga promotion in the mail I’d sit down and study it. I’d tear it
apart, cut it up and analyze it every single way possible. It’s a great way to learn.
What would be the first step in increasing average customer value?
So Phillip’s gone into a company or he owns the company and he wants to increase
customer lifetime value. There are three components.
• 	Order frequency – how often a customer orders from you.
• 	The average order amount – how much he spends each times he buys.
• 	How long he continues buying from you.
Those are the three things that determine lifetime value. If you improve each one of these
numbers incrementally, the total value of that customer rises in order of magnitude.
Order frequency – what does that have to do with? How can I increase order frequency?
You can look at the number of promotions a customer receives per month. You can look at
what happens with his other contacts with the company.
What happens when a product is delivered? What kind of sales material is in the package?
What happens when he calls customer service? How are they scripted either to increase
his order size or to get another order from him?
What happens if he receives a bill from the company? What kind of stuffer is in the
bill making an offer to him? How can you use discount coupons or some kind of a
membership program that gives him credit for the number of times he orders?
What can you do in those areas? There are a whole lot of ideas in those areas that we’re
going to be looking at.
The next one is the average order amount. This gets to offer structure. In other words, a
client has a health product. You can order a 3 month, 6 month or one year supply. What
do you want to do here? You want to get everybody to buy the one year but they won’t.
People buy a three month supply, so what happens next?
How do you bump them on the phone, with a letter that goes out after the purchase, or

-- 16 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
a package stuffer that gives them another 15 days to still get the 6 month price if they up
their order now?
There are a lot of ways to do that. The more you look at the businesses processes the
more opportunities you’re going to see to increase that average order amount.
Finally, longevity. This has to do with customer retention. I would start with customer
service. A lot of people when they think about retention think renewal programs, we want
you back programs for expiring customers and so forth.
Instead, take a look at customer service and how they handle cancels. How are they
handling complaints? There’s an essential truth about this whole business building thing.
That is that every time there is contact with a customer there’s an opportunity for a sale.
Every time.
Yet people are running great companies and high volumes of calls into customer service
asking questions. They’re not all negative calls. Some people want to know when their
product will get there, how to use something, or before ordering I want to know this or the
other thing. They just answer the questions.
That kind of stuff drives me nuts. That’s the opportunity to increase the order amount or
to make another sale, right? On the negative side, when somebody is calling in to cancel
that’s a great opportunity to make a friend for life and to make sure that customer will
come back to you forever.
The old thing is you should be willing to spend. The cheap guys out there will say you
should be willing to spend as much to keep a customer as you originally spent to get him. I
think you should be willing to spend five times more.
You’re not keeping that customer just for this month or this purchase. You’re keeping that
customer for the next year, 5, 7 or 10 years. Over that period of time he’s going to spend
a lot of money with you. When the rubber meets the road, if you were there for him, he’s
going to be there for you and be with you a long time.
Longevity is really big. We’re going to do a lot more on that in the weeks ahead. I hope
that helped. We’re all the way to 33. We’ve got three more.
Stuart is writing an advertorial. He’s got a nice front end. He’s giving away advice, he’s
giving away secrets on the front end of this advertorial – do this, do that, achieve the
required or desired result. Here’s his question.
Now I’m at the point where I’ve just proven I know what I’m talking about
by bringing value to their lives. How will I transition in the copy to buy my

-- 17 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
product?
The answer, Stuart, is really pretty simple so long as you haven’t started out in the wrong
direction. The tips that you’re giving need to be tips from either a premium or from the
product itself. We’re assuming an information product here.
Your transition is simple. “People are always asking me where in the world I come up
with this stuff. ‘These tips are great!’ Everything I just told you was taken directly from the
pages of XYZ.”
Typically I like my first step after giving them a bunch of mind blowing information is to
have it come from a freebie. That gives me the opportunity to give them more tips and say,
“That’s also from your free copy of this.”
Then provide a bunch of bullets that are very tantalizing and make them want to know the
answers to those questions. “That, too, is from your free copy of XYZ.”
Only after I’ve totally blown them away with the value of the information in their free stuff
that I say how to get your free copy of XYZ. Then you go into, “These great premiums are
yours free just for accepting a risk free trial of my product.”
That’s a great way to transition it. There are other ways if you’re doing something else.
For example, the tips you’re giving them are not from the product you’re selling. Maybe
it’s a three dimensional product. Maybe you’re selling a garden tiller but the tips are about
gardening. Basically the transition is still into the premium which gives them more tips
about gardening that they get free for trying your product. That’s typically how it’s done,
Stuart. I hope that helps.
From Larry –
I’m overwhelmed with too much to read. What’s the solution?
Rich Schefren and I are buddies and after spending some time with Rich he thinks I’m a
creep because I don’t read much. I spend so much time writing I don’t have a lot of time to
read. He always knows the six principles of this or the four do’s of that – all of these great
sounding theories and stuff.
I don’t know any of them but I use them all of the time. They’re common sense or I do it
instinctively. I tell you what, Rich blows me away. The depth of his knowledge is humbling.
I’ve decided that my solution for this problem, Larry, is I’m going to take a speed reading
course. I’m already a fast reader. That’s what Rich has done and the guy is phenomenal.
He burns down a big long book in a couple of hours. I’m going to do that. So that’s my

-- 18 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page  of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
solution.
Having said that, that isn’t where your question is. You want to know how to read fewer of
these publications, the ones that are going to bring the greatest value to you. Besides our
own, Bencivenga Bullets, that’s Gary Bencivenga’s newsletter. You can Google all these
guys and they’ll come right up.
Bencivenga’s Bullets. Gary publishes very sporadically but everything he does is solid
gold. If I were you I’d go over to his site. I think it’s www.BencivengaBullets.com and sign up.
Then every time he publishes you’ll know.
The Golden Thread, which is AWAI’s ezine, I’ve enjoyed. Bob Bly is a buddy of mine
and really does a lot of great things on his site. You should check him out. John Forde’s
Copywriter Roundtable is great. He’s one of those guys that’s gets better as he goes
along. I really love his stuff.
I interviewed the guy who started Copyblogger last week I think. You talk about a
character. You’re going to be getting that CD soon. He’s a brilliant guy. You should check it
out. They have lot of great articles and fantastic archives.
Michel Fortin’s Copywriter Board is also great. There are a lot of great threads there, and
Warrior Forum of course. I’m always encouraging you guys to expand your chops beyond
copywriting into the whole spectrum of direct marketing and you’ll really see a lot of great
threads on Warrior Forum to help you do that. There is some good copy threads there, too.
Final question -
What new ideas and programs are you working on now or planning for the
future? What could we look forward to with excitement?
My mission this year is to get as many copywriters as possible fully educated in direct
marketing techniques. Then they’ll have a whole other product line that they can walk into
a company with.
One of the problems that’s happening right now is you go through all the training, become
a copywriter, and maybe you write a few pieces. Maybe you only have spec sign ups. Has
anyone taught you when a client gives you a strategy for a piece how they came up with
that strategy?
Has anyone shown you how when you go to meet with a client you can give him two, three
or five ideas for his company as a whole, not just his copy, that will so blow his mind that
he’s champing at the bit to get you on his team? How to spot where the money is falling
through the cracks in that company? How to spot the most explosive opportunities?

-- 19 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page 20 of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
On our blog I told a true story about a friend of mine who called the owner of a small direct
response company that had about 20,000 odd customers on their file. He gave him one
idea and said, “If you like the idea I’ll write the promotion for free. All I want is a percentage
of what we sell and we’ll send it to your customers.”
The client agreed and the numbers were just phenomenal. I looked them up when I
wrote the thing and if I remember right it was $10 million dollars over a weekend that they
brought in.
The company owner was more than happy to give away the percentage because it was
intangible. It was something out there on the horizon and he’d never done anything like
this before. He had no idea he was talking about cutting a check to this copywriter for
millions of dollars when he said yes.
When the money came in it was so huge. He had to sit down and write the check and he
was so thrilled about it because he had five times more money than the copywriter made
from the promotion.
That’s the kind of explosive income potential there is when you take a good solid
understanding of copy and add it to a good solid understanding of direct marketing
technique. Opportunities are buried in every single company out there.
We made it to 36 and it’s only 13 minutes after. That’s amazing. We have a couple of
questions that have come in.
Cheryl says, “Clayton, I rather accidentally happened to stumble upon you
and this group. Oh, my heavens, I’m absolutely loving this. I so appreciate
your sincerity and genuineness.”
Thank you, Cheryl. We have a lot of fun on these things. I hope you make a lot of money
with the ideas that you’ve heard here.
Stuart says, “Totally off the point. Many different market viewpoints. It seems
like you have nailed where your markets are right now. While you can’t give
financial advice, which newsletters would you recommend for us to use?”
If you’re talking as an investor, Stuart, I think the best thing to do is get a variety of views.
I think it’s important to see the positive and negative side of what’s going on. The bears
I know out there who are saying it’s the end of the world and the United States as an
economic power and we’re all going to hell and it’s going to be terrible, they tend to focus
on every piece of bad news that comes out. They tend not to see so much the 180 million
Americans that go to work every day, that build equity in their bosses’ companies, or the

-- 20 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 2
Transcript 	Page 2 of 2
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
power of the government to add liquidity to the economy.
Of course the bulls out there are completely ignoring the negative.
The best advice I can give is get a balanced view from all sides. Then use Ockham’s
Razor which says 99% of the time the simplest explanation is the best and try to sort it out
for yourself.
Thank you all very much and this was a lot of fun for me. I hope it was helpful for you. You
all have a great week. Go out and make some big money. Take care.
This ends Session 2 of Module 7, Makepeace Secrets Exposed.
