-- 21 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I want to welcome everybody and thanks for being with us today. We thought it would be
a good idea today to directly address questions regarding the Partnering Model and other
things that you guys have been sending us questions on.
It’s one thing for me to sit around and talk to you all about partnering or copywriting or
various things. To be able to answer your questions directly is really great. I don’t think that
we take advantage of it enough.
That’s what we’re going to do today. You all have sent in some great questions. Let’s go
ahead and kick it off and get started.
Question #1:
The first one is from Cheryl. Cheryl is evidently in the process of trying to sell the concept
of partnering to a client. She wants to know how to describe to the client the value that she
would bring to that partnership.
When you’re talking to a client about the idea of partnering, the benefits to the client can
be fairly obtuse or fairly vague and very hard to sell unless you first determine what is it the
client needs.
I’m really thinking along the lines of you as the professional marketer looking at a company
and saying, “Wow! If we just added an ezine to the mix,” or “If we added email campaigns,”
or “If we added television or radio or direct mail, we could really take this to the moon.” It’s
your professional opinion of what he needs.
Another thing entirely is what the client wants. I have a client, for example, that came
to me and said, “I need a ‘Welcome Aboard’ series for six of my products. I need more
direct mail pieces. We’re not testing enough in the mail. I need in-house online marketing
campaigns every month.”
He was very clear on what he thought he needed. He needed PPC campaigns and
banners and email name acquisition campaigns online – all kinds of things. He had a
whole laundry list.
The thing is, if you own a company you have two competing emotions. The first one is you
believe in your heart that if everybody knew that you were there, your company would be
absolutely exploding in size. Hopefully you think your products are that good.
The business owner you’re talking to has that frustration going all the time. He’s frustrated
and probably a little bit amazed that his company isn’t growing faster.
The second thing is that he is painfully aware of what needs to be done in many cases to

-- 22 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 2 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
get the word out or to bring more customers into the fold. He’s got this long laundry list.
One of the great truths of life – and especially business life – is that there are always more
great ideas for promoting a company than there are people to execute them.
That’s especially true when it comes to copywriting because – as I say so often –
copywriting is the scarce resource. Clients we work with can develop products all day long.
They can find people to develop products for them easily. They can hire raw recruits out of
college and have them develop products for them. The scarce resource – the hard person
to find – is someone who can write great copy to sell those products.
If you as a copywriter who’s becoming a rainmaker who’s trying to set up partnerships can
identify, 1) what the client really needs from your professional standpoint, and 2) let him
tell you what he wants, then it becomes a fairly easy matter when you’re putting together a
proposal of showing him what you’re going to provide for him every single month in terms
of copy, strategy, campaigns, and so forth.
Show him how by answering into a partnership with you a couple of things happen. First
of all, he has instant access to a copywriter who can take his company to the moon. To get
on board with a good copywriter takes time sometimes, which is one of the problems. Not
only is it hard to find good copywriting talent, but to get on the writing schedule of a good
writer – you may have to wait a month or two to get your project done.
Secondly, a good writer is going to charge you an arm and a leg for every project. As a
copywriter or partner working with a company, you have some security because you know
that client’s going to be with you every month. He’s going to be giving you assignments,
and he’s going to be paying you. That gives you the freedom as a copywriter to give him a
break on what you charge for some of these projects.
I’ll give you a good example of that. For one of my clients – in fact, we were just talking
about this today – for probably ten years he has paid my standard fee, which is a $25,000
advance. It’s a freelance fee. It’s a $25,000 advance to write a direct mail package and
$50 per thousand royalty. For every piece he mails, he has to pay me a nickel.
We’ve been doing business that way together for years. Even through the partnership
model I maintained those rates. Recently we restructured our arrangement where the vast
majority of my incentive comes from selling things to existing customers. It becomes in my
best interest to bring in as many new customers as we can for this client.
The more I charge him on the front end, the fewer new customers he’s going to be able
to bring in because he’s paying me instead of spending that money to mail direct mail
packages and so forth. It made sense to me to say, “Look, I want to give you a huge break
on these direct mail packages. I’m not going to charge you a $25,000 advance. I’ll charge
you $4,000 to write a direct mail piece. That’s it. But I still need the $50 per thousand

-- 23 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
royalty.” That’s a $21,000 advance on every new test panel that he gets from me.
I, on the other hand, know that he’s going to be more aggressive in the mail, be testing
more things, and have a better chance of getting big winners in the mail. When these
pieces work, we take the $50 per thousand. Of course, because I work through an agency,
the copywriters I have here at the agency stand to make a chunk of that money.
That’s a long way around to say that when you’ve got the security of an ongoing client
relationship, and if you’ve incentivized a particular point in his marketing process (like I’m
incentivized on the back end), there are things that you can do for that client that are going
to ultimately make you money that you don’t have to charge for at all – or at least not have
to charge as much for. That’s another advantage for a client to partner with you.
The third one is a little harder to sell, but most clients get it right away. Whenever they
work with a copywriter, they have to teach that copywriter all about their business. They
have to teach them what’s worked, what hasn’t worked, who their prospect is, and the
benefits of their product that seem to resonate best with those prospects. There’s just a big
learning curve that has to happen. It has to happen every single time a new writer jumps
into the mix.
As a partner, you grow to know your client’s company as well as he does. It’s more than
just head knowledge. You have the visceral experience of going through successes
and failures with them. It saves a great deal of time in terms of creating his marketing
campaigns. At the same time, it raises his response. You’re working from a position of
knowing him and what’s worked before.
The final advantage really is that because you’re so intimately involved in his business and
you see his response rates over time to see what’s working and so forth, you’re perfectly
suited to be able to come in and suggest new products to promote.
Your role is going to expand from just copywriting now to copywriting and maybe
involvement in the graphics process – the design layout process – to product
development.
Also, as things develop, you are going to see chokepoints in his company that are keeping
him from quickly testing new concepts or developing new products. You will find yourself
involved in the organizational side as well.
Those are all great advantages.
The bottom line, Cheryl, is this: my suggestion is to let your client tell you what he needs
and what he wants, and then offer to give it to him. Then show him the advantages in
terms of time savings, cost savings, and the level of expertise.

-- 24 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Question #2:
The next question is from John. This is more a copywriting question. John is concerned
about believability and skepticism. He says:
Believability is critical to convince the prospect to try or buy the product.
He wants to know what steps must be taken to allay the skeptical mind of the potential
buyer so that the offer is compelling and they order it.
John, the one thing that I’d like to suggest that you do is to spend some time in The Total
Package archives. I’ve written at great length about the use of credibility elements in sales
copy. You’re absolutely right. Our markets are all becoming more skeptical.
Next week’s issue of The Total Package is going to be about the most unique direct
mail piece I’ve ever received. It just came in. It’s a credit card promotion. On the outer
envelope, the return address says, “Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.”
That’s it. That’s the return address. When you open it up, the sales letter says,
Dear Clayton,
Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
The entire letter is nothing but ‘blah, blah, blah’ except there are little islands because
there are four things I want to know about your credit card, right?
I want to know what the introductory rate is, and there are certain things I need to know
about that card. The ‘blah, blah, blah’ is interrupted here and there during the letter with big
bold text of what the initial interest rate is and what the minimum payment is and the other
things that I need to know about this card.
I thought, “I can really empathize with the copywriter who wrote this.” Americans have
become so inured to marketing. I think skepticism is part of that. We’ve become so tired,
so fatigued just by the sheer volume of advertising and marketing that we’re getting on a
daily basis.
Three years ago when we started The Total Package, we did a calculation that showed
that the average American gets nearly 700 advertising messages directed at him every
single day – on the web, in the mail, in the newspaper he reads, on the television that he
watches. He’s seeing something like 700 advertising messages.

-- 25 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you are not the average American – if you make a little bit more money or if you’ve
expressed interest in a particular hobby or a particular pursuit, you can receive as many as
2,000-3,000 advertising messages a day.
Just sheer fatigue has us tuning out when it comes to advertising. Also there’s skepticism,
especially with the rise of the internet. Skepticism is at an all-time high.
John, I would suggest that you go to our archives and go through them, keeping an eye
out for articles that we’ve done – and some of these articles are about 20 pages long in
Word. Some of them are very detailed and very ‘how-to’ and so forth.
Let me just cover five ways to allay skepticism in promotions that you may be doing right
now. Again, there’s a lot greater detail on this in the archives.
1. 	I like to use third party sources a lot when quoting facts.
Instead of saying, “The inflation rate is four percent,” I want to say, “According to the U.S.
Department of Labor’s April report, the U.S. inflation rate is running at four percent per
year.” By invoking the name of the U.S. Labor Department, I’ve brought instant credibility
to the factoid that I just presented.
The same is true for doing a promotion for a health product. If inactivity is the number one
cause of heart disease in America, have the AMA saying it or some other credible source
that doesn’t have a dog in this fight.
Skepticism in direct mail and on the web – a lot of it – doesn’t come so much from the fact
that you’re offering a product. It comes from the fact that the facts that you present in your
sales copy are self-serving. I mean, it’s obvious you’re presenting a fact in order to sell a
product.
Every fact you present is suspect. Have a third party source. I have a quote that we picked
up a couple of weeks ago where Alan Greenspan says that oil prices will continue to rise
forever. Now my client doesn’t have to say that. The former Chairman of the Federal
Reserve said it. If that’s the case, then we have a product that can help you profit from
that.
2. 	Obviously customer testimonials.
It still amazes me how many times I see an otherwise good direct mail package that’s
either extremely light on testimonials from people who have enjoyed the product – or
maybe even totally devoid of them.
Make sure that’s on your checklist. Look for opportunities to use testimonials in the running

-- 26 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
text of your sales letter – maybe in a callout box, in every spread, on the order form, or on
the premium flyer.
Get testimonials if you have a free giveaway or a premium. Don’t think that you have to
just have testimonials about the product that you’re actually selling. Look for, solicit, and
get testimonials about the product, your spokesperson, the free gifts that they get along
with the offer, the quality of your customer service (“Your operators were so friendly and
helpful on the phone”),or about how fast your product was delivered after they ordered it
– all of the key points that are involved in this transaction.
Look for ways to use another source – a customer testimonial in this case – to drive home
the truth that all of this is indeed superior to what the prospect may have experienced
before.
3. 	Expert testimonials are also great.
As a copywriter, it takes time. If you’re moving from one project to another, doing one
project a month or every couple of weeks, getting a client to go out and solicit testimonials
from people respected in his field is not always easy to do. Making the suggestion – even
if it’s not something that’s going to be immediately available to you for use in your copy
today – will at least do a couple of things.
It will get him thinking about it, and hopefully he’ll actually do it. Secondly, the next time
you work with that client – if he’s a freelance client – those testimonials could be available
to you.
One of the best things that a financial or health publisher can do is to have a very active
and very successful experienced public relations team. Getting that team then, to have
your articles by your spokesperson placed in the relevant industry publications is a
great way to go. When they present your spokesperson’s viewpoint in their magazine or
newspaper or on their TV show, they have to introduce your spokesperson.
In order to introduce the spokesperson, they have to give his qualifications. When they
give his qualifications, they are validating those qualifications. They’re giving you a quote
that you can use.
I know that most of you have seen my financial promotions direct mail that I’ve done
over the years. You’ll see in there that I am shameless about the use of that. If Fortune
Magazine says that Martin Weiss is America’s #1 Investor Safety Advocate, that’s going to
be on every direct mail piece I do. That’s instant credibility.
It’s not self-serving. It was somebody else that said that about my spokesperson.

-- 27 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
4. 	Proof elements
Those of you who have been with me for a while are aware of the Enhanced Oral
Chelation direct mail pieces that I did starting several years ago with Health Resources.
The product is a chelating product called EDTA that bonds with calcium in your arteries.
By doing so, it causes the plaque that’s in the arteries to let go of the artery wall and to be
flushed away.
There’s a test that you can do where you can actually see this rogue calcium in urine. In
our direct mail promotion we actually had this little experiment that the prospect could do
himself at home when he got the product. He would take a beaker of urine and hold it up in
front of a light bulb after taking Oral Chelation for three or four days, and you could actually
see the calcium suspended in the urine.
That was a proof element. That was a demonstration.
We’ve done the same thing in showing how other health products work by using
illustrations that look like they were taken from Grey’s Anatomy. In those demonstrations,
we would show how Fibron in the blood causes clotting, and how clots can cause phlebitis
or even a stroke or a heart attack.
Showing the disease process with a demonstration is a very effective way to get past
skepticism.
5. 	Begin with a point in common
This is very simple reason. There’s a method that really is at the heart of everything that
we do. I mean, I learned how to do it when I was a kid. I was trying to convince my parents
to let me do something.
The way it works is you begin with a common touchstone. You begin with a fact or a point
of agreement – a fact that somebody already knows. This is something you don’t have to
sell.
If you’re an investor right now, we might say, “It’s no secret that the dollar is falling like a
rock.” Well, if the guy is an investor and he’s on the list that we go to when we try to sell
investors products, he knows that. Right off the bat, we’re on agreement on something.
Then you build on that fact. If x is true, then y is also true, right? You just bring him along
with you one step at a time.
You might say, “Right now oil prices are through the roof. Gold prices have moved up in
lockstep with them.” The investor is going to say, “Okay”.

-- 28 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Then you can go to a third party source. “Alan Greenspan says oil prices are going to
continue rising forever because supplies are dropping, demand is rising, and the dollar
is falling in value so it buys less oil.” What does that mean? That means that gold is also
going to keep rising forever. You have a chain of logic.
This item number five reason really is logic. If you can build a case so strong that it just
blows skepticism away, you’re pretty much home free. Really, that technique is the heart
of what we do of salesmanship in print: Reasoning with the prospect, helping him to see
things from your perspective, and then convincing him to take the recommended action.
I hope that helps, John. Be sure to check out the archives, though, because you’re going
to find a lot more on this. It’s a big one with me. You’re going to see how I love using either
a testimonial or some kind of a track record statement in the eyebrow above my main
headline so that right off the bat the credibility of my spokesperson is established.
Anyway, there are some great articles in there. I can’t recommend them highly enough.
Question #3:
Question number three is from Chris. Chris has a challenge here. He’s got an idea. He
thinks it would really help solve a major problem for a major Fortune 500 company. The
company is evidently getting kicked around by the competition. Chris has figured out a
way to stop the bleeding, turn the tables on the competition, and really get the company
growing again.
The second paragraph here is his problem. He doesn’t have a big ad agency behind him.
He wants to know:
Is there a way to get compensated for ads you write for this Fortune 500
company, which would likely have to be placed by their present ad agency?
He’s asking for advice. How would I approach this?
Chris, you’ve got a major problem here. It’s more than just placing the ad.
The thing is that these Fortune 500 companies have long-term exclusive contracts with ad
agencies. Some of the bigger companies have several ad agencies – maybe one for every
product line that they have. If you call any executive at the Fortune 500 company with an
idea, all they’re going to do is give you the phone number of their ad agency.
The ad agency has absolutely no interest in your idea. First of all, they have no idea how
to sell products to begin with, which is why they’re not direct response ad agencies. It’s

-- 29 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
why they’re general ad agencies.
Secondly, most of them have creative directors – graphic artists – supervising copywriters.
They just don’t get it. They don’t understand how to sell a product.
You’re dealing with the antichrist here. You’re dealing with people who have a vested
interest in not accepting your idea, not working with you, and who don’t really respect what
you bring to the table in the first place.
I think you see the direction that this is going. You have a better chance of winning the
lottery than to get a deal with a Fortune 500 company given those facts.
What I would do in your situation is if I have an idea for a company like that, rather than
going to the Fortune 500 company, I would figure out what are the mid-sized and smaller
companies that are competing with this Fortune 500 company that could use an idea like
this? Then I would approach those guys.
The smaller and mid-sized companies are far less likely to have the kind of agreements in
place that a Fortune 500 company would have.
The only ray of sunlight I could offer you is that if you had an insider at the company that
had the ear of the person who makes the advertising decisions, you might be able to get a
hearing for your idea. In most cases, the chances of them seriously considering it are fairly
small. The fact of the matter is that most of them aren’t to be that trusted anyway.
Remember, you’re dealing with venal, self-interested executives to whom stealing great
ideas is kind of the high point of their day. Look for the smaller more entrepreneurial
company – the hungry company – rather than these big beaurocratic bloated Fortune 500
firms.
There’s a reason why Fortune 500 companies don’t grow as fast as companies that are
one one-hundredth their size. It’s not just market saturation. It’s the entrepreneurial spirit
and the willingness to value new ideas.
I wish I had better news for you, Chris. That’s pretty much the way things are.
Question #4:
Next we have Gabrielle from the U.K. Interesting name, Gabrielle.
Gabrielle is looking to move to Spain or Brazil and would like to know how the partnering
techniques should differ for use in international markets such as U.K. or Spain.

-- 30 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 0 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
She goes on to say that the contracts I’ve presented really wouldn’t help in the U.K.
I have no way to know that, Gabrielle, I have to assume that the answer to that would
be ‘yes’, but the people in the U.K. and Europe that I’ve dealt with tend to be highly
entrepreneurial firms run by the founders that are just hungry for growth. Even when they
get huge, so long as the founder is still at the helm they tend to have that nature.
If they are like that, then they are looking for opportunities to speed up the marketing
process, improve the quality of the marketing, and to use the marketing to really grow the
company.
One company that springs to mind is Norman Rentrop’s company over in Bonn. Verilog
Norman Rentrop is a publisher. He’s a great guy – a workaholic. The company is famous
for its 2:00 in the morning board meetings. I can see Norman jumping at a deal like this.
I would assume the principles here – because of the benefits to the company – are pretty
much universal.
Going back to the question we just answered, though, the size of the company is key.
Also I’ve seen all the prime companies in my market niche – in the health and investment
niches – change over the years. I was there when Robert Kephart started KCI and when
Tom Phillips started Phillips Publishing and Chip Wood started Soundview and Martin
Weiss started Weiss Research and Howard Ruff started Ruff Times.
I saw all of these things from their earliest days. I also saw them years later – decades
later – when the original principals sold the company or retired and gave the reigns to
someone else. The difference in the aggressiveness and the genius of the marketing in
those companies changed when the founder left.
That’s another thing to look at when you’re looking at partnering. Look for a company
where the founder is still at the helm. Make sure that you’re talking with the founder – the
owner of the company – and not a middle management marketing person.
Keep in mind that the middle management marketing people have a vested interest in
keeping you out. When you come into the company, they have to give up some amount of
control and prestige and opportunities to impress the boss.
If you’re the guy coming in with all the great ideas and so forth, a lot of marketing people
are threatened by that. They’re going to try to kill the deal. Dealing with the owner of the
company is very important.
Gabrielle, as far as the contracts we’ve provided, you’re absolutely right. I don’t think I’ve
ever even seen a contract that was drawn up in the U.K. I’m sure the language is going to

-- 31 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
be very different, but the deal points in those contracts are worth thinking about.
We’re not trying to make sure that attorneys don’t have enough work by giving you those
contracts. You should always go to an attorney when you’re putting together any kind of
contract that is going to potentially represent a substantial amount of money to you or to a
client.
The deal points in the contract hopefully will be helpful to you.
Question #5:
Rich asked -
Suppose a copywriter has already written successful direct mail packages in
a catalog for a client but is not getting royalties or a percentage on the back
end to the house file. Since the client is accustomed to getting a free ride
– so to speak – what would you do now to convince the client to start paying
a profit percentage?
The main answer to that question is: If you’ve written something that has worked well for
a client and has worked better than what he’s used in the past, then you’re in a perfect
position to simply be unavailable.
Someone asked me this at the Power Marketing seminar. They asked, “How do you raise
your rates? How do you just raise your fees?”
I said, “Write a winner and then be suddenly unavailable.” That’s how Gary Bencivenga
did it. It’s how Jim Rutz did it. Jim blows into town, writes for three or four promotions, and
then disappears for a year or two. He’s involved in overseas ministries. He just disappears
for a year or two.
Then he blows back into town, sends an email to three or four of the clients he’s worked
with in the past, and says basically, “I’m in town for six months. I’m going to write four
packages. The fee is $100,000 flat cash in advance. If you want on my schedule, you’d
better contact me immediately because I’ve just sent this to a whole bunch of people.
Chances are, these four slots are going to be gone fast.”
It’s friendly. There’s no hype. It’s just truth. Everyone has seen this pattern over the years.
If you’ve written something that’s worked, you’re in the catbird seat. Becoming unavailable
or becoming at least harder to schedule writing time with is just a wonderful position to be
in.
The beauty of that is that quite often you don’t have to raise your rates. If the client’s really

-- 32 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 2 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
sold on the quality of your work and the incremental increase of response that you’ve
given him, you don’t have to raise your rates. The client will raise them for you.
Obviously I’ve done this a few times in my career. I mean, the first promotion I wrote I
charged $800 to write a direct mail promotion for an investment newsletter in 1979. $800
and I was doing it on a typewriter where I had to retype each draft from scratch.
I went from there to the rate that I charge right now which is $25,000 and $50 per
thousand. The quantities of these mailings can be huge.
We were talking earlier about demonstrations to establish credibility. That has gone out
now in well over 60 million direct mail pieces. My royalty on that was $50 per thousand.
That gives you an idea of the incremental increase in wages from $600-$800 for a direct
mail piece.
It’s an ongoing process. As you create winners, you bring your rates up behind you.
Haroon’s being a smartass. He just put a comment in here:
I was one year old when you wrote that first financial package.
Thanks for reminding me!
Anyway, I hope I made that point clear. You need to constantly be reviewing what your
rates are.
Another thing that I found was very helpful was: If I wrote a winner for client A, I would then
go to his competitors and make sure that they knew that I wrote that catalog or that direct
mail package. I would make sure that they knew that I wrote it.
What then happens is they immediately say, “Would you write something for us?” I
would then engage them in conversation about that. Knowing that I had a better shot at
negotiating a higher rate with my client, I would immediately beat feet back to Client A and
say, “Your biggest competitor over here just called and he wants me to write a direct mail
piece for him.”
That’s like fingernails on a chalkboard of a client. I think it makes them much more willing
to negotiate with you.
I hope that helps, Rich. It is a continuing process as you establish more and more winners
in your career to constantly be asking yourself, “What’s the next step?” in terms of
compensation.

-- 33 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Question #6:
Marcus has already gotten started in partnering. He’s looking at a potential payday of
over $500,000 Euros (if I put the period is in the right place), but he’s got some concerns.
His partner is good to him and he seems honest, but they’re very slow in getting their
marketing out.
Remember what I said earlier about seeing chokepoints in your client’s business? Already
Marcus has an opportunity there to show his value to the client by trying to help identify
what those chokepoints are and offering solutions to speed the marketing process.
Their list is much smaller than I assumed. Is this part of the learning curve?
Absolutely. Never assume anything. Get counts, Marcus, when you’re doing a deal with a
new partner.
Remember, you can keep them honest. You can find out who their list broker is and get
a copy of their data card. That will tell you exactly how large their list is. Or you can go
to the library and crack open the Standard Rate and Data Service for direct mail lists. If
their list is on the market and being rented – and most of them are if it’s a direct marketing
company – that will tell you how large their list was at the time that that particular edition of
the SRDS was printed.
The size of the file is absolutely crucial. I have to say that I kind of got caught up in this
thing. I had a national organization contact me six to eight months ago. They wanted me to
take over their marketing. My assumption was, “Holy cow! I know this organization. They
must have three or four million names!”
I asked the question a couple of times and never got a really good answer. Later on, after I
had expended a lot of effort with the organization, I found out that their list was only about
60,000 names. I have to say that if this had been a company that was selling financial
newsletters and they had 60,000 names, I would have been fine with that. I would have
been really excited about the back end potential and the commission potential on a list of
that size.
This wasn’t. This was a national organization – and you all would know the name of
the organization – and I was just dumbfounded when I found out that they only had
60,000 names. Of course, because this organization was dealing in much smaller dollar
amounts on each transaction, it was kind of a deal killer for me. I only found out after I had
expended effort.

-- 34 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Double-checking list sizes is absolutely essential. That’s kind of a side trip here.
Marcus’s question really is how long should he keep going with this client. I’m not really
clear about this last part, but he’s evidently invested over six weeks in work in this client.
Marcus, and I think this is good for all of us to keep in mind, you know the key to
partnering – and I do this when I’m negotiating and signing a partnering contract right at
the beginning – is just to say, “We’re going to try this. I’m going to do my best and you’re
going to do your best. If at any time it’s really not working for either of us, we need to have
another conversation like this and fine-tune this agreement as we move along.”
Open that door and have that honest forum so that you can go and say, “This part of the
deal is working for me. This part over here isn’t. One of two things has to happen. Either
this problem is going to get solved or I’m going to have to change my fee structure to
compensate for it.”
Having the freedom to have that kind of conversation with a client is absolutely essential
– especially if you hope to be working with that partner over the long haul. I’ve done that
numerous times with all of the companies I’ve partnered with.
For example, it sounds to me like you really like the partner. You say that he’s kind and
he’s honest. Your frustration is that they’re very slow. This happens a lot. You work your tail
off to get something done, and then it just languishes with the client rather than getting out
there in the marketplace where it can start generating money for everybody.
If that’s a problem and it’s with a client and the client likes you and you like the client, then you need to just call
him up. Say, “I’d like to come to your office and spend a little time with your people. Then I’d like to come back
to you and talk about how we could speed up the marketing process.”
A lot of companies haven’t really thought through what it costs them when each campaign
they do takes a week or two weeks longer than it otherwise could have. One way to
multiply sales and sales revenues and profits at a company is just going in and saying,
Right now, your turnaround time from the time that you test a promotion to
the time you rollout is eight weeks. What we’re going to do in this meeting is
cut two weeks off of that. We’re going to do that one day at a time.
We’re going to go through and we’re going to say, ‘If we do this a
little differently, we can save a day. Over here we can save two days.’
Everybody’s ideas are welcome. Come hell or high water, by the end of this
meeting we will have cut our turnaround time on our direct mail rollouts by
two weeks.

-- 35 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you do that and the guy is now doing six mailings a year and you cut two weeks off of
each of those mailings, you’ve saved twelve weeks. That means he’s got at least one
and maybe two additional mailings this year, which means an incremental increase in the
number of new customers he’s going to get – or if they’re house files mailings, revenues
he’s going to generate. Time is money in a very real way when it comes to marketing.
Again, Marcus, you can look at this as glass half full here. If you can go into the client
and solve this problem and create a marketing machine for him that really clicks and that
moves his stuff through the pipeline more quickly so that it’s generating money more
quickly, you’ve just increased the value that you bring to the table in this partnership.
I personally have done this kind of thing for a client. He said, “How am I going to pay you
for this consulting time?”
I said, “No problem. Give me 10% of your company.”
He said, “Okay.”
I got stock options for 10% of the company that ultimately were worth hundreds and
hundreds of thousands of extra dollars for me. The amount of time I invested solving his
marketing problems, slicking up the process and everything, was probably a few weeks’
time over a period of three or four years. It was a good return on investment for me and it
made him a fortune, too.
Look at it as an opportunity. Having said that, if you go to the client and you say, “This is
costing us both a boatload of money” and you can’t get a response from him, at the end of
the day you have to do a cost/benefit analysis.
You have to look at the client and say, “If I had known the things I know about this client
now when I originally set up the partnership, would I have accepted this relationship on
these terms?” If the answer is no, then you have two choices. You can either end the
relationship or you can change the terms.
Once again, it’s simply a matter of laying your cards on the table with the client. Your client
understands that you’re a business man just like he is. He understands risk/reward and he
understands return on investment.
If your promotions are languishing someplace in his shop rather than being used where
they could be generating money for the both of you, not only is it in his interest to solve
that problem, but he’s going to respect you for bringing it up because – after all – you’re a
business owner just like he is. He can feel your pain.
Sometimes it’s hard to be a grown-up on these things and precipitate a crisis, even a mild

-- 36 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
crisis, in someone’s life by saying that something has to change here, but it’s always the
best way to go.
Question #7:
Marcus also had another question. I was going to delete this one, Marcus, because you
already had one in here. It’s not about partnering. It’s just a darn good question.
Does it make sense to work on two big projects simultaneously? It seems
that good thoughts always come for Project A when you work on Project B
and vice versa.
Marcus, the answer is that each of us has to find the work process that works best for us.
For me personally, there are few eternal truths about that work process. I find that on each
project that I’m confronted with, my work process changes somewhat.
The one truth that seems always to come back to me is that I’m most ready to write a new
promotion for a product the minute that I’ve just completed writing a promotion for that
product. I have tons of unused ideas that were great but just didn’t work with the original
promotion I did.
For me personally, working on two big projects simultaneously does not work. I need to be
able to really eat, sleep, and breathe a project over a period of time to get my best work
done on it. You need to do what works for you.
Parris Lampropoulos sits down at the beginning of every project and writes 300-400
fascinations about the product. I couldn’t possibly do that. Carline Anglade-Cole sits down
with a product and comes up with dozens of pages of the craziest, most off the wall ideas
you’ve ever seen in your life – stuff that would never work in a million years. But she
pursues every single one of those ideas because quite often those ideas will yield other
ideas that will work.
Every writer’s process is different. My process is different on every single promotion I do.
Just find what works for you and go for it. There’s no right or wrong way to do this. The
only good or bad in this entire process is established at the end of it when you’re counting
the money.
Question #8:
Rick asks:
What, if any, changes do you make in your copy if your audience is the
officer of a medium-sized company? My son thinks you need to be formal

-- 37 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
and business-like. I say that they all put on their pants the same way. Who’s
right?
There’s a principle here, Rick, that extends to your sales copy as well as to proposals
when you’re trying to pitch a company for a partnership deal. You need to respect your
prospect’s time.
We had a copywriting workshop in my offices here last week. The one theme that kept
coming up over and over again was: You’ve got to communicate quickly and efficiently.
You’ve got to get your message across in a way that respects the fact that your prospect
– in this case, an officer of a company – you want to be doing business with has extremely
limited time and probably limited attention span.
When I was working with Blanchard and Company, we sold the company to a subsidiary of
General Electric. Every single month the head of each division had to put together a report
that would ultimately go to GE headquarters and would be reviewed by Jack Welch.
Jack’s rule of thumb was that you ought to be able to give him everything he needed to
manage the company in less than one page. I respected that. I thought that was really
cool.
He had developed a methodology and knew exactly what he needed me to tell him about
my division in order for him to be on top of it and to be making the right decision. Smart
business men will look at things in that way.
How does this apply to your situation? We’re going to get into this next time in great detail,
but people who teach business in college are doing it because they can’t do business.
I mean, these people don’t have a freakin’ clue – most of them. They’ve never run a
business. All they did was read a damn book sometime in their lives about how to do
business and some other idiot a generation ago gave them an ‘A’ on a test so they got to
graduate.
Marketing and business professors in most colleges aren’t worth the powder it takes to
blow them to hell. If they could do it, they’d be doing it and making millions. They can’t do
it. Therefore, they’re teaching a whole bunch of poor suckers who paid a huge amount of
money to go to their stupid class how to do it wrong.
They’re going to show you how to do these massive proposals and to use all kinds of
verbose pontifications and stupid phrases that make no sense like ‘facilitatal synergistic
relationship’ and crap like that.
What it comes down to is: “Here’s what you need to do. Here’s what I’ll do for you. Here’s
what I think will happen when it’s all over and the dust settles and we count the money.

-- 38 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Here’s how I want to be paid for it. Fair enough, sign here. Not fair, fine. I’m going down
the road.”
Keep it simple. Get straight to the point. Don’t be formal, but be professional. Does that
make sense? Formal to me connotes flowery business language and basically bullshit
– forgive me.
Don’t be formal. Be practical and pragmatic. Demonstrate wisdom by getting straight to the
point and I think you’ll be miles ahead of the game.
There’s a lot more we’ll be saying about this next time. I’m getting excited about our next
session because it’s going to be a lot of fun looking at that kind of stuff.
I’m sorry about my little rant there about professors, but come on. That’s why when MBA’s
come out of college they don’t go to work at the top of organizations. They still have to put
in their time and learn the ropes and unlearn a lot of the crap they learned in college.
That was the editorial, by the way. It’s only fair to tell you that I’m a high school dropout, so
my views are certainly not unbiased.
Question #9:
Are there certain industries or niches that are more suitable or open-minded
to partnering?
We kind of discussed this – or at least what my answer is going to be – a little bit earlier.
I don’t think it has as much to do with the niche as it does with the size of the company,
whether the owner is actually running the company, and how committed they are to
growth.
You find a company that meets those criteria. The owner – the guy who’s going to make
the lion’s share of the money and who’s running it – because of that is eager or maybe
even desperate to grow the company. The older he is, the more desperate he is because
he wants to retire someday – either sell the company or put somebody else in charge and
move on.
The company needs to be of a size where they don’t already have lots of prior
commitments, especially exclusive commitments with advertising and marketing agencies
that would preclude you striking a deal with them. I think those things have a lot more to
do with it than what the niche is.
I’ve had arrangements with companies that made desserts and water heaters and sold
rare coins and vitamins and all kinds of stuff. It can pretty much move across any niche

-- 39 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page  of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
you can think of. Just make sure that the company is suitable in those other ways.
Question #10:
Here’s a question that actually came from our staff. We’re going to wrap up in just a
second.
I’m a brand new copywriter. What’s the quickest way to boost my confidence,
make money, and get a partnering gig going?
Work. You need to work. You need to write every day. You need to emulate things that are
working. Harun’s hilarious because he’s got the postman pretty much timed here at the
office. That mail doesn’t have time to cool off in the mailbox before Harun’s out there rifling
through it to see if there’s any good direct mail out there he can study.
You’ve got to eat, sleep, and breathe this stuff. That’s step one.
Step two is: Create a winner or a series of them.
Step three is to convert – and it is a process – from flat-fee writing to royalty writing to
partnering.
I hope that helps.
Let’s see what other questions they cooked up for us here. Oh, I like this one. We’ll end
with this one.
Question #11:
What are some of the downfalls of the partnering model? If it’s so great, why
isn’t everyone doing it?
You know, I honestly don’t know. I’ve been tempted to publish my tax returns so that my
friends who are freelance copywriters will finally get a clue.
The thing is that a lot of them have been to my home and they see how we live and they
know that the incremental difference in income between my model and the way they’re
doing it with the freelance model, but change is difficult.
A lot of these writers have been doing this for 10, 20, or 30 years. They’ve been doing it
the same way. They’ve been moving from client to client. They’ve been doing the new
learning curve with every single project and all of the hassles of freelancing. It’s just what
they’re used to. If they’re happy with that, God bless them. Who’s to judge them?

-- 40 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 20 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I think the number one obstacle really is that change isn’t easy. As I said earlier, you have
to go in and precipitate a crisis of some kind. That’s really an overstatement. You have to
say to a client that you love and that you’ve worked with on a particular basis for some
period of time, “I want to change our deal.” A lot of people are uncomfortable doing that.
It’s amazing to me that all of these great salesmen in print that I know that I count as my
friends are really not comfortable selling themselves. To go into a company that they’ve
been comfortable working with in a particular way for some period of time and having to
demonstrate that they have a lot more value to bring to the table than just good copy is
something that they just would rather not do.
Frankly, if you’re Carline or Parris or one of these other great freelance writers, you’re
making $800,000 or $900,000 or maybe $1 million a year anyway. They seem to be pretty
happy with the way they’re going.
For me – in about two to three hours every morning – I’ve done the work that I’m going to
do for the day that makes my income for me. I’m done for the day. The rest of my day is
spent doing stuff for the profit center an d for other things that don’t pay me anything but
just to keep me busy. It’s actually less work for me over the long haul and more money, so
I love it.
Having said all that, I just want to say that there are some downfalls:
1. 	Not everybody that you structure a partnering relationship with is going to turn out
to be a great partner. There are going to be problems of chemistry between you
and the underlings of the company. If you do, you deal with the owner.
2. 	Some underlings are going to resent you. Some will sabotage you. Some will tell
outright lies in order to try to turn your client or your partner against you. It does
happen.
3. 	There are going to be times when the market turns against you and your income
will fluctuate, but that happens in freelancing too.
I’m really struggling here to give you a totally honest answer and give you a long list of
problems that you can encounter.
Like any relationship, not all of them work. Not all freelance relationships work either.
Frankly, the benefits outweigh any problems that I could come up with by such a long shot.
It’s hardly worth struggling over.
Question #12:

-- 41 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 2 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I guess I’ll take one more. I talked about helping Parris structure his first partnering deal. I
did it with Jim Punkre too and with others.
Can you give us more details?
Sure. In each case, a copywriter called up and said, “I love this partnering model. I’m
making great money now but I think there’s an opportunity here. I’ve got a client that’s
interested. How do you set them up?”
I just ask them, “What are you doing for the client now? Where do you see the
opportunities? What is it the client wants out of this?” – all of the things that we’ve talked
about on this call. Then we compartmentalize what he’s going to do.
If he’s going to do name acquisition on the front end, we talk about the best way to get
paid for that. We also talk about, “How are you going to handle the overflow?”
Parris, for example, brings in copy cubs. He mentors them. I’ve met a couple of them, and
Parris has got a knack for this. The people he’s found are just fantastic.
“How are you going to handle the overflow when there’s more work than you can do? Are
you going to do renewal campaigns? If so, how are you going to get paid for those? Are
you going to do back end marketing? If so, what kinds of products does he have? How are
you going to get paid for those?
“How about PPC and online name acquisition? Are you going to be involved in those? Wow! There’s not much
money in those. How are you going to get paid for those?”
That’s an opportunity to say, “Look, I’ll do your PPC ads for you for free. Every morning I’ll wake up and I’ll
check the news, if there’s anything going on out there that has something to do with a product you’re selling.
“In the financial market, for example, I’ll see that the dollar is down big time today. I’ll write some more contra
dollar pay per click ads or something else that I think would fit into the theme of what’s going on out there right
now. Maybe Obama’s pulling ahead in the polls so you do a bunch of PPC ads on ‘Obama killed the stock
market. Take this poll and find out.’
“I’ll do all that for free because I really want your PPC to be huge. I won’t charge you for that, but I really need
you to pay me for this other thing over here. In order for it to make sense for me to do those for you (and it
will only take an hour a day – no big deal) I want to make sure that instead of getting 8% on the back end, I’m
getting 10%,” or something else like that.
You look at all the different work you’re going to do, how to get paid for each, what you can lay on the table as
a freebie that the client gets for signing the contract, and then we just kind of worked it all out. It’s a methodical

-- 42 of 43 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 7 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 3
Transcript 	Page 22 of 22
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
process of thinking it all through.
Alright, guys. I hope that helped.
Again, thank you all. Goodbye.

-- 43 of 43 --
